title: SEGMENT 2 — Writers, erasers, readers, and the chemistry of 5mC

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — Writers, erasers, readers, and the chemistry of 5mC
Let me build the picture of DNA methylation as a biological system.
There are three sets of proteins you need to know about, and the field calls them by a tidy trio of names: writers, erasers, and readers.
The writers are the enzymes that put the methyl group onto cytosine in the first place.
They're called DNA methyltransferases, abbreviated DNMTs.
There are three main ones in humans.
DNMT1 is the maintenance methyltransferase — its job is to copy the methylation pattern from the parent strand to the daughter strand after DNA replication.
Every time a cell divides, the genome gets copied, and every methylated cytosine on the old strand corresponds to a freshly unmethylated cytosine on the new strand.
DNMT1 follows the replication fork, recognizes the half-methylated CpG sites — methylated on the old strand, unmethylated on the new — and methylates the new strand to match.
That's how methylation patterns are inherited across cell divisions.
Without DNMT1, every cell division would dilute the methylation pattern by half, and within a few generations the cell would forget its identity.

DNMT3A and DNMT3B are the de novo methyltransferases — the enzymes that establish methylation patterns in the first place, particularly during embryonic development, when the genome is being patterned from a relatively unmethylated zygote into the highly cell-type-specific patterns of adult tissues.
DNMT3A and 3B don't need a half-methylated template; they can methylate fully unmethylated DNA.
They're the painters; DNMT1 is the copier.
Together, the three DNMTs maintain and establish all the methylation in the genome.
The erasers are the enzymes that remove methyl groups.
For decades, the field assumed that methylation was a one-way mark — that the only way to lose a methyl group was passive dilution, by failing to copy it during replication.

Then in 2009, Anjana Rao and others discovered the TET enzymes — TET1, TET2, and TET3 — which actively oxidize 5-methylcytosine into a chain of intermediates, starting with 5-hydroxymethylcytosine (5hmC), then 5-formylcytosine, then 5-carboxylcytosine.
The downstream intermediates get recognized by DNA repair machinery, which excises them and replaces them with a clean, unmethylated cytosine.
So TET enzymes provide an active pathway for demethylation.
The TET story is important for two reasons.
First, it overturned the simple picture of methylation as a static mark.
Methylation is dynamic; cells write and erase it actively.
Second, and this is the part that mattered enormously for the measurement methods we're going to discuss, 5-hydroxymethylcytosine — 5hmC — turned out to be a stable intermediate in its own right.
It accumulates to substantial levels in certain tissues, especially the brain and embryonic stem cells, where it can make up several percent of all cytosines.
5hmC has its own biology, its own readers, its own genomic distribution.
It is not just a transient stop on the way to demethylation; it's a distinct epigenetic mark in its own right.
And as we'll see in Segment 10, the original bisulfite sequencing chemistry could not tell 5mC and 5hmC apart, which created a problem the field is still working through.
The readers are the proteins that bind methylated DNA and recruit downstream machinery to silence transcription, compact chromatin, or do whatever the methylation is signaling.
The classic family is the methyl-CpG binding domain proteins, or MBD proteins — MBD1, MBD2, MBD3, MBD4, and MeCP2.

They have a small protein domain that specifically recognizes a methylated CpG, and they bring along other proteins — histone deacetylases, repressive chromatin complexes — that make the local chromatin closed and silent.
The most famous of the MBD proteins is MeCP2, mutations in which cause Rett syndrome, a severe neurodevelopmental disorder.
That's the clinical reminder that the methylation reading machinery is not optional decoration; cells whose readers don't work end up profoundly impaired.
So you have a complete writing, copying, erasing, and reading system for a single chemical mark on a single base in a four-letter code.
The system is genome-wide, cell-type-specific, and tightly regulated.
The marks are dynamic across development, stable within a differentiated cell, and increasingly disrupted in disease and aging.
And every one of those biological observations — when methylation happens, where it happens, how it changes — depended on having a method to actually measure 5-methylcytosine across the genome.
Which brings us to the measurement problem.
Here is the conceptual challenge.
Methylation is a single chemical decoration on a single carbon of a single base.

The methylated and unmethylated forms are otherwise chemically nearly identical — they base-pair the same way with guanine, they get copied by polymerase the same way, they sit in the chromosome at the same position.
Standard DNA sequencing, of any flavor, will report both 5mC and unmethylated C as plain old "C" because that's what the polymerase reads.
To detect methylation, you need a chemistry — or, in the case of nanopore, a measurement modality — that distinguishes the two.
For two decades, that chemistry was bisulfite.
Let's go meet it.
